title-group.article-title: Poor Association Between Clinical Characteristics and Seropositivity in Children With Suspected Long COVID—A Single‐Centre Study

aff.named-content: Department of Paediatric Infectious Diseases, Astrid Lindgren Children's Hospital
Department of Women's and Children's Health
Department of Allied Health Professionals, Theme Women's Health and Allied Health Professionals
Department of Clinical Science, Intervention and Technology (CLINTEC)
Department of Psychology, Stress Research Institute

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Acta Paediatrica published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd on behalf of Foundation Acta Paediatrica.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Aim
sec.p: We aimed to compare characteristics and clinical presentation of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) antibody positive or negative children attending a specialist outpatient clinic for suspected paediatric long COVID.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A cross‐sectional study was conducted of 113 children and adolescents enrolled between 1 December 2020 to 14 September 2021 in a multidisciplinary programme. Clinical and epidemiological data were collected with standardised interviews and laboratory tests including SARS‐CoV‐2 spike antibody measurement.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A serological link to SARS‐CoV‐2 infection was found in 52%. Most patients (94.7%) reported several symptoms. Fatigue, post‐exertional malaise, dizziness, nausea, headache, and concentration difficulties were the most common. Seronegative children had a higher number of individual symptoms. School absence and drop‐out from leisure activities was substantial in both groups with higher numbers for the seronegative group. Self‐reported health was low in both groups.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Children attending a specialist paediatric long COVID clinic experienced multiple symptoms and poor self‐reported health. The symptomatology was similar regardless of serological status, implying multifactorial causes. A multidisciplinary assessment of this cohort was essential considering the broad spectrum of symptoms displayed and their substantial impact on everyday functioning.

institution-wrap.institution: Henry and Ella Margareta Stahl Foundation
The Pediatric Research Foundation at Astrid Lindgren Children’s Hospital

fn.p: Funding: This study was funded by Henry and Ella Margareta Stahl Foundation and The Paediatric Research Foundation at Astrid Lindgren Children's Hospital.

list-item.p: Knowledge regarding long COVID in children is growing but the condition is still incompletely understood.
In this study children with or without antibodies against severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) were evaluated at a specialist clinic for suspected long COVID.
Both groups were characterised by multiple symptoms, substantial school absence and poor self‐reported health, implying multifactorial causes and a need for early multidisciplinary support.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Children infected with severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2) normally experience mild or no symptoms at all [1, 2, 3]. Severe disease, intensive care admission or death was very rare even in the early phases of the pandemic [4]. Although long‐term symptoms have been reported in children after COVID‐19, they seem less common than in adults [5] and may be difficult to distinguish from other conditions or pandemic‐associated symptoms [6, 7]. A 2022 meta‐analysis by Lopez‐Leon et al. estimated that up to 25% of children < 18 years infected by SARS‐CoV‐2 had symptoms at least 4 weeks after infection, the most common symptoms being fatigue, headache and dyspnea [8]. However, similar symptoms have been reported in children who have not had COVID‐19, questioning the specificity of these symptoms [9, 10, 11, 12]. Additionally, it is well known that viruses other than SARS‐CoV‐2, like Epstein–Barr virus, can cause long‐standing fatigue in children and adolescents [13]. We aimed to study if children with COVID‐19 antibodies, differed from seronegative children in symptom characteristics, school attendance and self‐reported health.

Following reports of children with long‐term symptoms after suspected COVID‐19 in the Stockholm area, a growing concern from families [14], and the need for more knowledge about the condition, we opened a multidisciplinary outpatient clinic in November 2020. A structured, standardised clinical assessment was developed. The study was carried out in the early phase of the pandemic, prior to immunisation practices started.

sec.title: Study Design, Participants, and Settings
sec.p: This study cohort comprised patients 0–18 years referred to a specialist outpatient clinic at Astrid Lindgren Children's Hospital in Stockholm, Sweden 1 December 2020 to 14 September 2021. All had long‐term symptoms after suspected, probable or confirmed COVID‐19 as classified by the World Health Organisation (WHO) case definition [15]. None of the patients were vaccinated against SARS‐CoV‐2 at the time of evaluation. Patients with multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children were not part of this cohort. All patients were referred to the clinic from their general practitioner or community paediatrician, except in 20 cases where the legal guardians directly contacted the clinic. All patients had long‐term symptoms in accordance with the WHO clinical case definition [16]. Data about the primary infection and long‐term symptoms were collected from the child and the guardian through a standardised, structured medical history inquiry with a specialist in paediatrics, see Appendix S1. This was followed by a predefined work‐up, including a broad panel of laboratory tests, physical examination, exercise testing and structured interviews with a psychologist and a medical social worker. Health‐related quality of life was assessed with EuroQol‐5Dimension‐Youth (EQ‐5D‐Y), a self‐report questionnaire that has been validated in several countries, including Sweden [17]. Data regarding previous health was checked against the patients' medical records. Exercise testing results, including follow‐up testing, and psychosocial screening results will be reported elsewhere. Background characteristics variables were age, gender, time from acute infection to long‐term symptom onset, other somatic diseases, and earlier neuropsychiatric and psychiatric illness. Outcomes were health state on the day of visit, number of symptoms, school absence and leisure activity changes.

The study was approved by the Swedish Ethical Review Authority (approval no. 2021.02082) and all guardians provided written or digital informed consent. The study design flowchart can be found in Appendix S2.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: We conducted several regression analyses to assess the relationships between various covariates and outcome variables: school absence, EQ‐5D VAS scale, leisure activities, and number of symptoms.

sec.title: Descriptive Statistics
sec.p: For continuous variables we present mean and interquartile range (IQR) to summarise the data distribution. For categorical variables we present frequencies and percentages to give an overview of the distribution across the categories.

sec.title: Linear Regression Models
sec.p: For continuous outcome variables EQ‐5D VAS scale and number of symptoms, a multivariable linear regression was conducted to explore the combined effects of the covariates with antibodies as main exposure variable.

Diagnostic checks for the linear regression models were performed. Residual diagnostics, including tests for normality (Shapiro–Wilk test) and homoscedasticity (Breusch–Pagan test), showed no violations of the linear regression assumptions. Additionally, the variance inflation factors indicated no significant multicollinearity, and the residuals appeared to be normally distributed with no evidence of autocorrelation.

sec.title: Logistic Regression Models
sec.p: We applied a multivariable logistic regression model for the binary categorical outcome variables school absence and leisure activity.

Standard diagnostics for the logistic regression models were performed, including checks for model fit and residuals. The results indicated that the models met the necessary criteria, ensuring that the assumptions for logistic regression were satisfied. The statistical software used was R version 4.1.0.

sec.title: Baseline Characteristics
sec.p: Table 1 describes baseline characteristics and outcomes of 113 children (69% girls) between 7 and 18 years. The mean age at presentation was 13.1 years and 65% of the children were older than 12 years. Age did not differ by gender. Antibodies to SARS‐CoV‐2 were measured using a quantitative method aimed at the receptor‐binding domain of the S protein. Seropositivity was present in 52% of the children. There was no statistical association between antibody and gender (p = 1.00). Inflammatory markers, including CRP, ESR, albumin and ferritin were consistently normal. An underlying somatic disease was present in 42.5%, more commonly in seronegative children (p < 0.05). The most common was asthma, observed in 19.5% of the children. A neuropsychiatric or psychiatric disorder was present in 23.9%, 18.5% in seronegative and 28.8% in seropositive, not significant. Ten patients had ADHD, seven had an anxiety disorder and five had autism spectrum disorder.

caption.p: Population characteristics of children according to their SARS‐CoV‐2 antibody status.

sec.title: Health‐Related Quality of Life, School Absence and Leisure Activities
sec.p: Self‐reported, health‐related quality of life determined with EQ‐5D‐Y visual analogue scale (VAS) was 51.1 ± 21.0 on a scale between 0 and 100. Linear regression failed to demonstrate an association between antibody status and health‐related quality of life. School absence was more common in seronegative (83.3%) than in seropositive children (61.0%), with a similar pattern for leisure activities, which was completely interrupted in 44.4% of seronegative and 22.0% in seropositive children. Logistic regression models showed reduced odds of school absence, (odds ratio (OR) 0.37, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.13–0.98) and interrupted leisure activities (OR 0.31, 95% CI 0.10–0.87) in children with antibodies compared to children without antibodies.

sec.title: Symptoms Characteristics
sec.p: More than one long‐term symptom was reported in 94.7% of the children.

The median number of reported symptoms per subject was 10 (interquartile range 7–12, range 0–20). The number of symptoms was significantly higher in children lacking SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies compared to those with antibodies (p < 0.05). Multivariable linear regression confirmed this association, with a coefficient estimate of −2.60 (95% CI −4.03 to −1.18, p < 0.001), indicating fewer symptoms in children with antibodies.

The mean time between acute SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and start of long‐term symptoms was 1.82 ± 2.26 months and time from infection has been included in the multivariable regression models. The relationship between the presence of antibodies, gender and reported long‐term symptoms is listed in Figure 1. Fatigue was the most common symptom, present in 92.6% of seronegative and in 91.5% of seropositive children.

caption.p: Symptom presentation in percent of the entire cohort (n = 113). Boys without SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies (black bars), boys with SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies (dark grey bars), girls with SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies (grey bars) and girls without SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies (light grey bars).

sec.p: Apart from boys being more likely to report diarrhoea (p = 0.05) there were no gender differences in symptom presentation. Heart palpitations were reported in 47% and no difference was noted between antibody positive and negative children. Those lacking antibodies however, more frequently reported sore throat (p < 0.05), chest pain (p < 0.05), intermittent fever (p < 0.05) and rash (p < 0.05). Crude and adjusted ORs for the included variables are shown in Appendix S3.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study aimed to compare symptoms, health‐related quality of life, school absence and leisure activities in SARS‐CoV‐2 antibody positive or negative children with suspected long COVID. Inclusion was finalised before general vaccination against SARS‐CoV‐2 was initiated for Swedish children and teenagers. Self‐reported health was low in both groups with no gender differences. The results, assessed with the EQ‐5D‐Y VAS scale are in fact lower than those reported from Swedish children with chronic diseases like asthma, diabetes and epilepsy [18]. School absence was substantial and more common in the seronegative group. More seronegative children also reported that they had quit their sports or leisure activities, possibly reflecting a more pronounced symptomatology in this group.

National case control studies from Denmark and the UK focused on identifying long‐term symptoms in children in the general population following a positive or negative polymerase chain reaction (PCR) or antigen test for SARS‐CoV‐2 [9, 10, 11]. In the British CLoCk study, 30.3% of children aged 11–17 years with a positive test and 16.2% of children with a negative test had at least three symptoms 3 months after testing [19]. Girls, older children and children with underlying poor physical or mental health in both groups, had a higher risk of having multiple symptoms. They may be more prone to present to a long COVID clinic such as ours. Notably, 23.9% of all children in our group had a previous neuropsychiatric or psychiatric diagnosis. Of these, 13.3% had ADHD or autism spectrum disorder compared to 6.3% of all children 0–17 years in Stockholm 2015–2020 [20]. These subgroups of children may be at increased risk of post‐viral long‐term symptoms but may also be more sensitive to changes in daily life such as home schooling and stressful events in the family. Girls predominated in both our study groups, in line with most other studies on long COVID in children [8].

The studies above reported fatigue as one of the most common long COVID symptoms. Regardless of antibody status, more than 50% of our children also reported post‐exertional malaise. This is a condition of worsening fatigue following even minor physical or mental exertion. Post‐exertional malaise is also frequently associated with myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), commonly triggered by a viral infection [21]. Similarly, both ME/CFS and long COVID can cause dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system, causing a group of symptoms under the name postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome [22]. ME/CFS was shown to be strongly associated with a past SARS‐CoV‐2 infection in adults [23]. Still, only a few cases have been described in adolescents [24]. This may reflect that some of our patients were infected with other viruses than SARS‐CoV‐2 leading to similar long‐term symptoms. It has been speculated that similar biological mechanisms cause ME/CSF and long COVID [25, 26]. Independently of antibody status, we found heart palpitations and breathing problems to be common symptoms.

Our study had several limitations. Certain data on symptom characteristics were missing for some participants, making the data sets incomplete. This may have influenced the findings. Most evident is however that, since PCR was not readily used in the early phase of the pandemic, PCR data are lacking. Instead, we used the WHO Case definition for COVID‐19. These criteria are more subjective and may not accurately define COVID‐19 disease. All patients were tested for SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies, which is suggested by Stephenson as part of the definition of long COVID if PCR or antigen testing is not available [11]. The development of SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies in children following COVID‐19 and the significance of this is however still under investigation [27]. Children in our seronegative group may still have had COVID‐19 but not seroconverted or may have had antibodies below detection level. It seems though as infection with the early variants like Wuhan and Delta, which dominated during the time of our study, results in higher antibody levels and function compared with Omicron infection [27]. Our findings may thus suggest that a considerable part of our patients under investigation for possible long COVID suffer from long‐term symptoms due to other reasons than COVID‐19.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: For children and adolescents, the pandemic had a tremendous impact on daily life, including school attendance, social life, family interaction and leisure activities. More research on the impact that the pandemic as such had on children, regardless of COVID‐19 infection status, is warranted [28]. Long‐term follow‐up studies in groups like the one studied here are also needed to appreciate the future impact on children and adolescents suffering not only from long COVID but tentatively also post‐viral long‐term symptoms in general. Irrespectively of the cause of long‐term symptoms like the ones presented here, acknowledging their multifactorial nature, and establishing multidisciplinary teams to support affected children and their families early may be a key to help the children back to a healthy life.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: S. Olsson‐Åkefeldt: investigation, validation, writing – review and editing, writing – original draft, conceptualization. J. Luthander: investigation, writing – review and editing, validation. L. Anmyr: investigation, writing – review and editing. L. Villard: investigation, writing – review and editing. S. Arnason: investigation, writing – review and editing. M. K. Kemani: investigation, writing – review and editing. E. Wållgren: investigation, writing – review and editing. S. Röstlund: investigation, writing – review and editing. M. Tingborn: investigation, writing – review and editing. M. Pettersson: investigation, writing – review and editing. E. George: investigation, writing – review and editing. M. Ryd‐Rinder: investigation, writing – review and editing. O. Hertting: conceptualization, investigation, funding acquisition, writing – original draft, methodology, validation, writing – review and editing, formal analysis, project administration, supervision.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
